U
Oracle Events
OE1: Look up Samira Tariq's contact details using `contacts_search_contacts` (query: "Samira" or "Samira Tariq") to confirm sender email (samira.tariq@moveops.com) for outgoing emails.
OE2: Search email for ExpenseBot pilot audit outcomes using `search_emails` (query: "audit" or "ExpenseBot" or "47 expenses" or "expense auto-categorizer" or similar).
OE3: Search email for ExpenseBot pilot pause and deployment context using `search_emails` (query: "pilot pause" or "auto-categorizer" or "disabled" or similar).
OE4: Search email for AWS March bill escalation using `search_emails` (query: "AWS" or "$42,870" or "March bill" or "infrastructure cost" or similar).
OE5: Search email for AWS root cause analysis write-up using `search_emails` (query: "root cause" or "Vantage Distributed" or "cost spike" or similar).
OE6: Search email for stipend receipt fraud / duplicate findings using `search_emails` (query: "stipend" or "duplicate receipt" or "receipt hash" or "TechComfort" or similar).
OE7: Search Slack for ExpenseBot pilot and engineering remediation discussion using `conversations_search_messages` (search_query: "ExpenseBot" or "pilot" or "expense auto" or similar).
OE8: Search Slack for AWS cost spike investigation messages using `conversations_search_messages` (search_query: "AWS" or "root-cause-aws-spike" or "Vantage" or similar).
OE9: Pull the full investigation thread from the root-cause-aws-spike channel using `conversations_history` (channel_id: "C009") for line-item breakdown, root cause attribution, and leadership follow-up actions.
OE10: Search Slack for stipend or duplicate receipt discussion using `conversations_search_messages` (search_query: "stipend" or "duplicate" or "receipt" or similar).
OE11: List engineering project issues using `linear_list_issues` (project: "Expense Auto-Categorization Engine" or query: "expense" or assignee filters) to discover all relevant tickets.
OE12: Retrieve individual issue details for the 7 key tickets using `linear_get_issue` (id: `issue_pilot_monitoring`, `issue_audit_expenses`, `issue_pipeline_hardening`, `issue_p1_vectral_config`, `issue_p1_mosaic_malformed`, `issue_launch_readiness_checklist`, `issue_stipend_duplicate_detection`). Each get_issue call returns the full issue state, assignee, and description.
OE13: List comments on key issues using `linear_list_comments` (issueId: e.g. `issue_audit_expenses`, `issue_stipend_duplicate_detection`, `issue_pilot_monitoring`, `issue_p1_vectral_config`) for audit results, duplicate-receipt forensics, root cause details, and remediation status notes.
OE14: Search Airtable stipend records individually for each flagged employee using `airtable_search_records` (base_id: `appMoveOpsOps001`, table_name: `Stipend Transactions`, field_name: `Employee Name`, value: `Kevin Tran`), then (value: `Andre Washington`), then (value: `Jordan Ekwueme`) to pull the individual flagged records with amounts, review notes, and anomaly flags.
OE15: Search QuickBooks for the March AWS infrastructure bill using `quickbooks_search_bills` (criteria: vendor or description matching AWS / Amazon Web Services / March 2026 as applicable).
OE16: Retrieve the full bill detail including line-item breakdown using `quickbooks_get_bill` (id: `bill-aws-march-001`) for EC2, S3, and RDS cost components.
OE17: Search CRM for each affected client company — pilot clients and stipend-fraud-flagged clients — using `crm_search_companies` (name: "Vectral Systems"), then (name: "Mosaic Robotics"), then (name: "BrightLoop Analytics") to get company IDs and context.
OE18: Search CRM deals for each affected client using `crm_search_deals` (company_id: `company_vectral`), then (company_id: `company_mosaic`), then (company_id: `company_brightloop`) to find active deals and assess client relationship risk.
OE19: Look up Elena Rostova's email using `contacts_search_contacts` (query: "Elena Rostova" or "Elena") for CEO status email recipient.
OE20: Look up Dmitri Volkov's email using `contacts_search_contacts` (query: "Dmitri Volkov" or "Dmitri") for follow-up email to detection work owner.
OE21: Look up Lena Bjorkstrom's email using `contacts_search_contacts` (query: "Lena Bjorkstrom" or "Lena") for follow-up email to remediation work owner.
OE22: Update stale engineering issues with current status from the investigation using `linear_update_issue` (id: issue ID, description or state fields as appropriate) for multiple outdated tickets (e.g. issue_pilot_monitoring, issue_audit_expenses, issue_launch_readiness_checklist, issue_stipend_duplicate_detection).
OE23: Send Elena Rostova the consolidated status email using `send_email` (sender: samira.tariq@moveops.com, recipients: elena.rostova@moveops.com, subject and content summarizing ExpenseBot remediation, AWS spike, stipend receipt findings, CRM pipeline risk, and open items).
OE24: Send Dmitri Volkov a follow-up email using `send_email` (sender: samira.tariq@moveops.com, recipients: dmitri.volkov@moveops.com, content covering what is being reported to the CEO regarding the audit findings and ENG-220 detection work).
OE25: Send Lena Bjorkstrom a follow-up email using `send_email` (sender: samira.tariq@moveops.com, recipients: lena.bjorkstrom@moveops.com, content covering what is being reported to the CEO regarding ENG-214, ENG-215, ENG-217 remediation work).

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

📋 Rubrics
Rubric Results
✓ Pass ✗ Fail Click any row to expand justifications
# Criterion Run #1Run #2Run #3Run #4Run #5Run #6 Pass Rate
1 An email was sent (via send_email) to elena.rostova@moveops.com containing a consolidated engineering status update, and the tool returned success.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The trajectory shows a send_email call with recipients=['elena.rostova@moveops.com'] which returned a success result with email_id 'bc86340800bd4fe2b4a4b6e07047e5a7'.
✓Run #2The agent called send_email with recipients including elena.rostova@moveops.com and the tool returned a success response with email_id 'b977d63a55224016842cb552b2f3159c'.
✓Run #3The trajectory shows send_email was called with recipients=['elena.rostova@moveops.com'] and the tool returned a successful email_id ('2107f6348077403eb2a62b60681cdaad').
✓Run #4send_email was called with recipients ['elena.rostova@moveops.com'] and subject 'Engineering Status Update — ExpenseBot Pilot, AWS Cost Spike, and Stipend Fraud Findings', returning success with email_id '5ee691aaebaf4d89ae04819205c6b76e'.
✓Run #5The trajectory shows send_email was called with recipients ['elena.rostova@moveops.com'] and the tool returned success with email_id '8e6dd82bad5343ce81b7dab3d5ff5bd0'.
✓Run #6The trajectory shows a send_email call with recipients: ['elena.rostova@moveops.com'] (plus CC: ['marcus.thorne@moveops.com']) that returned a success response with email_id '86b3ee43f95245b5bfd0b9e168a4cda4'.
2 The email body to Elena (in the send_email content parameter) states that the pilot audit reviewed 47 auto-processed expenses and that overall accuracy was approximately 83% (or equivalent wording such as 39 of 47 correct).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✗✗✗ 50%
✓Run #1The email body explicitly states '47 total auto-processed expenses audited' and '39 correct decisions (83% accuracy)', satisfying both the scope and accuracy requirements.
✓Run #2The email body to Elena explicitly states 'Total expenses reviewed: 47' and 'Correct outcomes: 39 (83%)' confirming both the scope and accuracy figures.
✓Run #3The email body to Elena explicitly states 'full audit of all 47 auto-processed pilot expenses' and 'Correct decisions: 39 of 47 (83%)'.
✗Run #4The email to Elena mentions the audit covered 47 auto-processed pilot expenses, but does not state an accuracy percentage of ~83% or equivalent phrasing such as '39 of 47 correct.' The email describes 4 errors out of 47 without computing or stating an overall accuracy rate.
✗Run #5The email to Elena mentions the full audit of 47 expenses and 4 errors (2 false rejections + 2 false approvals), which equals ~91.5% accuracy (43/47), not the approximately 83% (39/47 correct) required by this criterion. The email does not state 83% accuracy or equivalent.
✗Run #6The email body states the audit covered '47 auto-processed pilot expenses' but never states overall accuracy as approximately 83% or equivalent phrasing like '39 of 47 correct'. The email only lists specific errors without computing overall accuracy.
3 The email body to Elena (in the send_email content parameter) reports approximately $885 in financial exposure from incorrect pilot approvals (Mosaic Robotics), referencing the three out-of-policy auto-approvals (e.g., standing desk overage, coworking membership, coworking day passes).
outcome
 ✓✗✓✗✗✗ 33%
✓Run #1The email body reports 'Financial Damage: $885 total confirmed exposure' and lists all three Mosaic false approvals (Andre Washington's desk $1,200, coworking $350, and Kenji Nakamura's coworking day passes $135) with the malformed JSON type root cause.
✗Run #2The email reports total financial damage as '~$1,550+' but does not specifically state ~$885 for Mosaic Robotics false approvals. The itemized amounts in the email ($400 standing desk overage, $350 coworking) do not add up to $885 and no $885 figure is given.
✓Run #3The email body to Elena states 'Total financial exposure: $885' and details the three Mosaic false approvals including standing desk ($1,200 overage of $400), coworking membership ($350), and coworking day passes ($135).
✗Run #4The email to Elena reports '$1,550+ in confirmed incorrect payouts' for Mosaic (combining $1,200 desk and $350 coworking), not approximately $885. It also only references two false approvals, not three (no separate coworking day passes item). The ~$885 figure and the third item are absent.
✗Run #5The email to Elena reports '$1,550+' in financial exposure from Mosaic false approvals and describes only 2 auto-approvals (standing desk and coworking membership), not three auto-approvals totaling approximately $885 as required. The numbers and count differ from the criterion.
✗Run #6The email reports '$1,550+' in financial exposure from Mosaic false approvals, not the approximately $885 specified by the criterion. Additionally, the email only describes 2 false approval items (standing desk $1,200, coworking membership $350/mo) and omits the coworking day passes item that would make the third out-of-policy approval.
4 The email body to Elena (in the send_email content parameter) explains Vectral Systems false rejections as stemming from incorrect default template limits / silent fallback in the policy ingestion pipeline (not the client's true policy limits).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email body explains Vectral false rejections as 'caused by silent fallback to default template limits in the policy ingestion pipeline', directly identifying the root cause.
✓Run #2The email clearly explains Vectral false rejections: 'the policy ingestion pipeline silently fell back to default template limits when it hit a merged cell in Chloe's spreadsheet. Vectral's custom limits were never loaded.' This matches the silent fallback/default template root cause.
✓Run #3The email body to Elena explains Vectral false rejections as 'The ingestion pipeline silently fell back to default template limits when Vectral's spreadsheet row had a merged cell,' matching the silent fallback / incorrect default template limits root cause.
✓Run #4The email explicitly states: 'Root cause: Silent fallback in the ingestion pipeline — a merged cell in Chloe's spreadsheet caused a parse error, and the pipeline silently substituted default template values instead of aborting.' This clearly identifies silent fallback to default template limits.
✓Run #5The email body to Elena explains the Vectral false rejections as caused by 'A merged cell in Chloe's source spreadsheet caused a silent parse error; the ingestion pipeline fell back to default template limits instead of aborting,' which directly matches the criterion's requirement.
✓Run #6The email explicitly explains: 'Ingestion pipeline silently fell back to default template when a merged cell in Chloe's spreadsheet caused a parse error. Config deployed with wrong limits.' This matches the 'silent fallback to default template limits' root cause.
5 The email body to Elena (in the send_email content parameter) explains Mosaic Robotics false approvals as stemming from malformed policy configuration types (e.g., excluded categories stored as a string instead of an array and/or max_amount stored as a string breaking numeric comparison).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email body states Mosaic false approvals were 'caused by malformed JSON types: excluded_categories stored as string instead of array, max_amount stored as string instead of number', covering both type issues.
✓Run #2The email explains Mosaic false approvals as 'Malformed JSON types in Mosaic's policy config — max_amount stored as string "800" instead of number 800, and excluded_categories stored as string instead of array.' This clearly covers both criteria variants.
✓Run #3The email body to Elena explains Mosaic false approvals as 'Malformed JSON types in Mosaic's config caused the PolicyValidator to skip exclusion checks' with 'max_amount stored as string instead of number, excluded_categories stored as string instead of array'.
✓Run #4The email explains: 'The max_amount field was stored as a string "800" — JavaScript lexicographic comparison let $1,200 pass' and 'The excluded_categories field was a string instead of an array — Array.isArray() check failed, exclusion logic was bypassed entirely.' This matches the criterion exactly.
✓Run #5The email to Elena explicitly states for Mosaic: 'max_amount was stored as string "800", so JS lexicographic comparison passed' and 'excluded_categories was a string instead of an array, so Array.isArray() check failed silently,' matching the malformed JSON types root cause.
✓Run #6The email states: 'excluded_categories stored as string instead of array (exclusion check skipped), max_amount stored as string causing lexicographic comparison (1200 <= "800" = true).' This correctly identifies malformed JSON types as the root cause.
6 The email body to Elena (in the send_email content parameter) reports the March AWS bill at approximately $42,870 versus a typical monthly baseline of approximately $14,300 (or describes the bill as roughly three times baseline).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email body explicitly states 'March AWS bill: $42,870' and 'Normal monthly baseline: ~$14,300', with 'Overage: ~$28,570 (~3x baseline)'.
✓Run #2The email to Elena states 'March AWS bill: $42,870' and 'Normal monthly baseline: ~$14,300' and 'Increase: ~3x ($28,570 overage)', directly satisfying this criterion.
✓Run #3The email body to Elena explicitly states 'March AWS bill: ~$42,870' and 'Normal monthly baseline: ~$14,300' and '~3x (~$28,570 overage)'.
✓Run #4The email states 'March AWS bill: $42,870' and 'Normal monthly baseline: ~$14,300', describing the bill as roughly three times the baseline, matching the criterion.
✓Run #5The email to Elena states 'March AWS bill: $42,870', 'Normal monthly baseline: ~$14,300', and '~3x ($28,570 overage),' matching the required figures.
✓Run #6The email explicitly states 'March AWS bill: $42,870' and 'Normal monthly baseline: ~$14,300' with the note it is a '3x increase'. Both numbers match the criterion.
7 The email body to Elena (in the send_email content parameter) includes at least one AWS bill line-item amount, such as EC2 at approximately $28,450.
outcome
 ✗—✗—✓— 33%
✗Run #1The email's AWS line-item breakdown section only describes what happened (EC2 scaled 4→18 instances, S3 had 47K uploads, RDS connections surged) without providing any specific dollar amounts per service. No figure like 'EC2 approximately $28,450' appears.
—Run #2No justification
